report.age.description: When each file was first introduced, when it was last modified, and how many times it changed in between. Old files that are still actively touched = mature core. Old files untouched for years = candidates for archival, deletion, or 'might be safe to delete' review.
report.authors.description: Who has contributed to this repository, how much each person wrote, and when they were active. Use this to spot the most active people, identify newcomers, or find authors who haven't touched the project in a long time.
report.bloat.description: Large files and committed artifacts that probably shouldn't be in git — minified bundles, build outputs, vendored dependencies, IDE configs, OS metadata. Removing or git-ignoring these (and rewriting history) shrinks the repository for everyone who clones it.
report.churn.description: How much each file has been rewritten over time — total lines added, deleted, and net change. High churn means a file keeps getting reworked, usually a sign of unstable design or unclear requirements. Files at the top deserve a closer look.
report.churn_pareto.description: Distribution of churn across files (the 80/20 rule applied to your codebase). Almost always a small fraction of files takes the lion's share of all changes. The summary row shows exactly how concentrated yours is — that small fraction is where to focus refactoring effort, code review attention, and test coverage.
report.complexity.description: Cyclomatic complexity per function — roughly, how many independent execution paths it has. CC under 5 is simple, 6-10 is OK, 11-20 is hard to test and reason about, 21+ is hard to even read safely. Functions at the top of this list are the best candidates to split into smaller pieces. `lines` counts executable code only (blanks and comments excluded) so a docstring-heavy function isn't flagged for length alone.
report.commit_velocity.description: Development tempo over time — weekly and monthly commit counts with total lines changed. Spot acceleration, slowdowns, and seasonal patterns. A steady cadence usually means healthy process; sudden drops may signal blockers or waning interest.
report.composition.description: Language breakdown at HEAD. Real code vs comment vs blank lines (not raw line counts) — block comments, nested comments, and shebangs are classified correctly via a 460-language knowledge base. Useful for sizing the codebase and spotting unexpected dominance (e.g. vendored JS crowding out Rust).
report.construct_churn.description: Per-function (not per-file) churn — how often each individual function or method has been changed. Bugs almost always cluster in 3-5 specific functions inside a hot file rather than spreading evenly. Use this to pinpoint exactly which function inside a busy file keeps breaking.
report.construct_ownership.description: Function-level ownership and bus factor — finer than file-level ownership. Even if a file has many contributors, individual functions inside it may have only one author. Bus factor of 1 on a critical function (e.g. payment processing) is a real risk even when the surrounding file looks healthy.
report.coupling.description: Pairs of files that tend to change together in the same commits. Strong coupling between files in different modules suggests a hidden dependency or missing abstraction — every time you touch A you're forced to touch B. Use this to find architectural seams to clean up.
report.debt_markers.description: Every TODO, FIXME, HACK, and XXX comment found at HEAD, enriched with git blame to show how long it has been sitting in the code and who wrote it. Oldest markers come first — these are the ones most likely to have been forgotten. If a TODO has outlived the problem it describes, delete it; if it still matters, schedule the work.
report.debt_markers.column.author: Author
report.fan_in_out.description: How files depend on each other through imports. fan_in = how many other files import this one (high = critical hub, change carefully because many files depend on it). fan_out = how many files this one imports (high = orchestrator, may be doing too much and worth splitting).
report.half_life.description: Per-file code longevity: what fraction of the current code was written more than {{ancient_months}} months ago. High percentage = stable, mature code that has stood the test of time. Low percentage = file is constantly rewritten, handle changes here with extra care.
report.health.description: Overall repository health on a 0-100 scale, built from five equally-weighted pillars: commit discipline, bus factor, refactoring debt, repo tidiness, and change concentration. Thresholds are opinionated — see scoring/health.rs for the rules. The 'Start here' list picks the highest-impact concrete actions from each pillar; 'Repo hygiene' lists cleanup commands (never run automatically).
report.hotspots.description: Files and code constructs that change very often AND are touched by many different people. Frequent change × many authors = high bug risk and reviewer fatigue. The top entries are your most likely refactor candidates — fixing one of these usually pays back across the team.
report.knowledge_silos.description: Files where one person wrote at least {{silo_pct}}% of the code, AND that person hasn't touched the file in the last {{idle_days}} days. If they go on vacation or leave the company, no one else can confidently change this code. Treat 'At risk' files as urgent knowledge-transfer items — pair-program a change, write docs, or split the file.
report.large_sources.description: Source files (handwritten code, not markup or data) sorted by executable code lines. Anything over 1500 code lines is a split candidate — it's hard to navigate, review, and test. An enormous file (5k+ code lines) is usually either auto-generated (consider moving to a build step instead of committing) or a 'god module' that has accreted too many responsibilities.
report.module_coupling.description: Like 'coupling' but at the directory/module level (depth {{module_depth}}) instead of per-file. Strong coupling between modules that 'shouldn't' be related (e.g. billing ↔ auth) reveals architectural smells: a leaky abstraction, a shared concern that should be extracted, or a piece of logic that lives in the wrong place.
report.outliers.description: Files that are unusually risky on two fronts at once: they change very often AND have many different authors. Both signals together almost always mean accumulated bugs, unclear ownership, and high review cost. These are your top refactor / split candidates.
report.ownership.description: Who 'owns' each file (wrote the most lines), how many people contributed, and the bus factor — the minimum number of people that would need to leave for the file to lose more than 50% of its knowledge. Bus factor of 1 on a critical file is a real risk: if that one person leaves, no one else can safely change it.
report.patterns.description: When commits happen — broken down by hour of day and day of week. Reveals team work patterns. Lots of late-night or weekend commits may indicate burnout, deadline pressure, or single-person bottlenecks. A healthy team usually shows a clear weekday daytime pattern.
report.quality.description: Signals that hint at risky commit habits: very short messages ('wip', 'fix', 'typo'), mega-commits with thousands of lines (hard to review or revert safely), reverts (a previous commit was wrong), and merge mess. Lower numbers across the board mean cleaner history and easier debugging later.
report.test_ratio.description: How much of the codebase is test code versus production code, broken down by language. Test files are identified by naming conventions (*_test.go, *.spec.ts, test_*.py, *Test.java, etc.) and test directories (tests/, __tests__/, spec/). A higher test ratio generally correlates with fewer regressions and safer refactoring.
report.succession.description: Per-file author succession: was the original author still active recently, and if not, who took over? Files marked 'Orphaned' (no active successor) or 'Knowledge transfer needed' (single successor) have lost their maintainer — if something breaks, no one alive in the project knows the original intent.
bloat.recommendation.very_large_file: Very large — investigate; use Git LFS if needed
bloat.recommendation.minified_bundle: Minified bundle — should be build artifact
bloat.recommendation.build_output: Build output — should be generated, not committed
bloat.recommendation.rust_build_output: Rust build output — should be in .gitignore
bloat.recommendation.os_metadata: OS metadata — add to global gitignore
bloat.recommendation.ide_config: IDE config — usually not checked in
health.summary.refactoring_debt: {{very_high}} fns CC≥20, {{high}} CC 11-19, {{outliers}} outliers, {{stale}} stale + {{rotten}} rotten markers, {{huge}} huge + {{sizeable}} sizeable files
health.summary.change_concentration: top 20% of files carry {{pct}}% of churn (80% is textbook Pareto)
health.detail.mega_commits: {{pct}}% of commits touch 1000+ lines — hard to review or revert
health.detail.high_revert: {{pct}}% of commits are reverts — CI/review gates are leaky
health.command.enforce_conventional: Enforce conventional commits or a `commitlint` hook in CI
health.command.split_commits: Split large commits by feature/file before merging
health.command.require_review: Require green CI + peer review before merge
health.command.pair_review: Pair-review next change to this file with a second author
health.command.extract_branches: Extract branches into helper fns; add a dedicated test first
health.command.split_module: Split by responsibility; if auto-generated, move to a build step instead
health.command.resolve_marker: Resolve it or delete the comment — don't leave it to rot
health.hygiene.large_git_dir: .git is {{mb}} MB — consider Git LFS for large binaries
health.insight.team_note: each contributor adds resilience to the project
health.insight.ownership: {{well_owned_pct}}% of source files have bus factor ≥ 3 ({{well_owned}}/{{total_files}})
health.insight.ownership_note: higher is better — shared knowledge reduces risk
health.insight.coupling_note: tightly coupled — consider extracting shared concern
health.insight.module_coupling_note: module-level coupling — may indicate leaky abstraction
health.insight.hub_note: changes here ripple widely — extra test coverage recommended
health.insight.active_cleanup: net deletion — codebase is being actively trimmed
health.insight.growing_codebase: codebase is growing — watch complexity and test coverage
health.insight.hotspots_note: focus code review and testing effort on these files
health.summary.architecture: {{tight_couples}} tightly coupled pairs, {{hubs}} hub files, {{high_instability}} high-instability files
large_sources.recommendation.enormous: Enormous — likely generated or a god module
outliers.recommendation.god_file: God file + ownership chaos — split responsibilities
outliers.recommendation.high_churn: High churn — consider refactoring for stability
quality.recommendation.enforce_msg_lengt: Enforce min message length or conventional commits
quality.recommendation.squash_wip: Too many wip/fix/typo — squash before merge
quality.recommendation.split_mega: Large commits hurt review — split by feature
quality.recommendation.strengthen_review: High revert rate — strengthen CI / review gates
quality.recommendation.rebase_workflow: Consider rebase workflow for cleaner history
